content.text: The Azure Free Account gives new users $200 in credits for 30 days, 12 months of popular services at no charge, and a set of always-free services that never expire. It is designed to let you learn, experiment, and prototype without incurring charges — as long as you stay within the defined limits and the spending limit remains active.

attrs.alt: Azure Free Account three-tier benefit structure: $200 credit, 12-month free, and always-free services

content.text: The Free Account bundles three distinct categories of benefits, each with different rules about duration and scope.
Credit expires; spending limit kicks in if not upgraded
Specific services within monthly quantity limits
Reverts to Pay-As-You-Go rates when period ends or quantities exceeded
Specific services within monthly quantity limits
Limits reset monthly; never charge unless you exceed the free quantity

The 30-day credit and 12-month services run in parallel — both start at account creation. If you use the $200 credit within the first 30 days, the spending limit stops any further charges. After 30 days the credit expires regardless of how much was used.

The following services are provided free for 12 months within their monthly quantity limits. Exceeding any limit in a given month results in PAYG charges for the excess, unless the spending limit is active.
Equivalent to ~1 month of one always-on VM
Data transfer from Azure to internet

Microsoft updates the free services list periodically. Always check the official Azure Free Account page before planning around a specific service's free tier, as quantities and included services change.
Always-free services remain free indefinitely, even after upgrading to Pay-As-You-Go. Usage above the monthly limits is charged at standard PAYG rates.
Small web apps, APIs (no custom domain SSL on F1)
Multi-model NoSQL database for small workloads
5 users, unlimited private repos, 1,800 pipeline minutes
Source control, CI/CD for small teams
Inbound data transfer from internet to Azure
Only build minutes; storage is charged
Control plane is free; pay for worker VMs only

The spending limit is a guard rail unique to Free Accounts. It prevents charges by suspending your services when you exhaust the $200 credit. This is distinct from a budget alert, which only notifies you.
While the spending limit is active: Azure tracks credit consumption. When credit reaches $0, Azure suspends all non-free resources (VMs are deallocated, websites stopped).
You can disable the spending limit at any time to convert to Pay-As-You-Go billing — required before deploying any production workload.
You cannot re-enable the spending limit once disabled. It is a one-way door.
The spending limit does not apply to Marketplace purchases or reservations — those always bill the credit card directly.
Free-tier services continue running even after the spending limit activates; only paid-tier resources are suspended.

# Check current subscription offer type and spending limit status
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>?api-version=2022-12-01' \
  --query '{name:displayName, quotaId:subscriptionPolicies.quotaId, state:state}'

# quotaId for Free Account: 'FreeTrial'
# quotaId for PAYG after upgrade: 'PayAsYouGo_2014-09-01'

# Check credit remaining (requires Cost Management read access)
az consumption budget list -o table

# List current month usage and costs
az consumption usage list \
  --start-date $(date +%Y-%m-01) \
  --end-date $(date +%Y-%m-%d) \
  --query 'sort_by([].{Name:instanceName,Cost:pretaxCost,Currency:currency},&Cost)[-10:]' \
  -o table
The most common mistake with Free Accounts is accidentally running a VM for a full month and exhausting the 750-hour limit in the first week. Monitoring usage in the portal and via CLI prevents this.

# View VM run hours for this month (to track against 750-hour limit)
az monitor metrics list \
  --resource /subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/<vm> \
  --metric 'Percentage CPU' \
  --start-time $(date +%Y-%m-01)T00:00:00Z \
  --end-time $(date +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \
  --interval PT1H \
  --aggregation Average \
  --query 'value[0].timeseries[0].data[].average' \
  -o tsv | grep -v '^$' | wc -l

# The line count above gives approximate hours the VM was powered on

# Stop a VM to preserve free hours (deallocated = no compute charge)
az vm deallocate --resource-group rg-test --name vm-test-01

# Start it when needed
az vm start --resource-group rg-test --name vm-test-01

# Auto-shutdown: configure a daily shutdown to conserve free hours
az vm auto-shutdown \
  --resource-group rg-test \
  --name vm-test-01 \
  --time 2000 \
  --email you@example.com

The 5 GB free Blob Storage limit covers the stored data size, not the number of transactions. Operations like reads and writes are also capped (2 million operations). Storing log files or large assets can quickly exceed 5 GB.

# Create a storage account under the free tier
az storage account create \
  --name stmylearning001 \
  --resource-group rg-learning \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_LRS \
  --kind StorageV2 \
  --access-tier Hot

# Check total storage used across all blob containers
CONN=$(az storage account show-connection-string \
  --name stmylearning001 --resource-group rg-learning -o tsv)

az storage container list \
  --connection-string "$CONN" \
  --query '[].name' -o tsv | while read CONTAINER; do
    echo -n "$CONTAINER: "
    az storage blob list \
      --container-name $CONTAINER \
      --connection-string "$CONN" \
      --query 'sum([].properties.contentLength)' -o tsv
done

# Set lifecycle policy to delete blobs older than 30 days (prevents accumulation)
az storage account management-policy create \
  --account-name stmylearning001 \
  --resource-group rg-learning \
  --policy '{"rules":[{"name":"delete-old","enabled":true,"type":"Lifecycle","definition":{"filters":{"blobTypes":["blockBlob"]},"actions":{"baseBlob":{"delete":{"daysAfterModificationGreaterThan":30}}}}}]}'
Azure Free Account vs Other Starter Options

Azure for Students (azure.microsoft.com/en-us/free/students) does not require a credit card — just a verified .edu email address. It provides $100 credit and includes always-free developer tools. Students should use this over the standard Free Account.

When ready to remove the spending limit and run production workloads, upgrade the subscription in the Azure portal. Upgrading is irreversible for the spending limit but the subscription itself can later be changed to other offer types.

# Upgrade is done via the portal: Subscriptions -> your free sub -> Upgrade
# After upgrade, verify the offer type changed from FreeTrial to PAYG:
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>?api-version=2022-12-01' \
  --query 'subscriptionPolicies.quotaId'

# After upgrading: existing resources continue running uninterrupted
# 12-month free services continue until their 12-month window expires
# Always-free services continue indefinitely

# Set a cost budget to catch unexpected spending after removing the spending limit
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name budget-monthly \
  --amount 20 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date $(date +%Y-%m-01) \
  --end-date $(date -d '+1 year' +%Y-%m-01) \
  --notifications '[{"enabled":true,"operator":"GreaterThan","threshold":80,"contactEmails":["you@example.com"],"thresholdType":"Actual"}]'
Practical Lab: Deploy a Free-Tier Web App
This walkthrough deploys a Node.js web application using only always-free and 12-month-free resources. All services used remain within the Free Account limits.

# Create a dedicated resource group for the lab
az group create \
  --name rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --location eastus \
  --tags Purpose=FreeAccountLab Budget=zero

# Verify
az group show --name rg-free-lab-eastus -o table
Step 2: Create an App Service Plan (Free F1)

# F1 is always free — 10 apps allowed on shared infrastructure
az appservice plan create \
  --name plan-free-lab \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --sku F1 \
  --is-linux

# Verify the SKU
az appservice plan show \
  --name plan-free-lab \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --query '{SKU:sku.name, Tier:sku.tier}'

# Create the web app with Node 18 runtime
az webapp create \
  --name myapp-free-$(date +%s) \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --plan plan-free-lab \
  --runtime 'NODE:18-lts'

# Get the default hostname
az webapp show \
  --name myapp-free-<timestamp> \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --query defaultHostName -o tsv

# Deploy a simple inline app (no git required)
az webapp up \
  --name myapp-free-<timestamp> \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --plan plan-free-lab \
  --runtime NODE:18-lts \
  --sku F1

# Create a storage account (LRS, standard — qualifies for 5 GB free)
STORAGE_NAME=stfreelab$(openssl rand -hex 4)
az storage account create \
  --name $STORAGE_NAME \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_LRS \
  --kind StorageV2

# Create a container for app assets
az storage container create \
  --name assets \
  --account-name $STORAGE_NAME \
  --public-access blob

# Get the connection string for the web app
CONN=$(az storage account show-connection-string \
  --name $STORAGE_NAME --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus -o tsv)

# Wire it into the web app settings
az webapp config appsettings set \
  --name myapp-free-<timestamp> \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --settings STORAGE_CONN="$CONN"

# Clean up lab resources when done
az group delete --name rg-free-lab-eastus --yes --no-wait
Using Azure Functions Under the Free Tier

Azure Functions has 1 million free executions and 400,000 GB-seconds of compute per month — permanently. This is the most compute-efficient free service for event-driven workloads like webhooks, scheduled jobs, and API backends.

# Create a storage account for Functions runtime (needed by all Function Apps)
az storage account create \
  --name stfuncstorage$(date +%s | tail -c 6) \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_LRS

# Create a Function App on Consumption plan (Consumption = always-free tier)
az functionapp create \
  --name func-free-demo-$(date +%s | tail -c 6) \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --storage-account stfuncstorage<suffix> \
  --consumption-plan-location eastus \
  --runtime python \
  --runtime-version 3.11 \
  --functions-version 4 \
  --os-type Linux

# List deployed functions
az functionapp function list \
  --name func-free-demo-<suffix> \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  -o table

# View current month execution count via Application Insights (if connected)
az monitor metrics list \
  --resource /subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-free-lab-eastus/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/func-free-demo-<suffix> \
  --metric FunctionExecutionCount \
  --start-time $(date +%Y-%m-01)T00:00:00Z \
  --end-time $(date +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \
  --aggregation Total \
  --query 'value[0].timeseries[0].data[].total | sum(@)'

The Cosmos DB free tier provides 1,000 Request Units per second (RU/s) and 25 GB of storage permanently. One free tier account is allowed per Azure subscription, and it must be enabled at account creation time — it cannot be added to an existing account.

# Create a Cosmos DB account with the free tier enabled
az cosmosdb create \
  --name cosmos-free-$(date +%s | tail -c 6) \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --locations regionName=eastus failoverPriority=0 isZoneRedundant=false \
  --enable-free-tier true

# Confirm free tier is active
az cosmosdb show \
  --name cosmos-free-<suffix> \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --query '{FreeTier:enableFreeTier, State:documentEndpoint}'

# Create a database and container (uses the 1000 RU/s from free tier)
az cosmosdb sql database create \
  --account-name cosmos-free-<suffix> \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --name mydb

az cosmosdb sql container create \
  --account-name cosmos-free-<suffix> \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --database-name mydb \
  --name items \
  --partition-key-path '/id' \
  --throughput 400

# Check storage used
az monitor metrics list \
  --resource /subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-free-lab-eastus/providers/Microsoft.DocumentDB/databaseAccounts/cosmos-free-<suffix> \
  --metric DataUsage \
  --aggregation Total \
  --query 'value[0].timeseries[0].data[-1].total'

The 1,000 RU/s free tier is shared across all databases in the account. If you create multiple databases each with 400 RU/s, you only pay for the RU/s above 1,000. Keep total provisioned throughput at or below 1,000 RU/s to stay free.
With a Free Account, setting up cost visibility before the credit runs out ensures a clean transition to PAYG billing. Configure cost analysis, budgets, and anomaly alerts from day one.

# View current month cost by resource type
az consumption usage list \
  --start-date $(date +%Y-%m-01) \
  --end-date $(date +%Y-%m-%d) \
  --query '[].{Meter:meterDetails.meterName,Cost:pretaxCost}' \
  -o json | python3 -c "
import json,sys
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
by_meter = {}
for r in data:
    m = r['Meter'] or 'Unknown'
    by_meter[m] = by_meter.get(m,0) + float(r['Cost'] or 0)
for k,v in sorted(by_meter.items(), key=lambda x:-x[1])[:10]:
    print(f'{v:8.4f}  {k}')
"

# Create a budget alert at $5/month (safe threshold for free account)
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name budget-free-alert \
  --amount 5 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date $(date +%Y-%m-01) \
  --end-date $(date -d '+13 months' +%Y-%m-01) \
  --notifications '[{"enabled":true,"operator":"GreaterThan","threshold":50,"contactEmails":["you@example.com"],"thresholdType":"Actual"},{"enabled":true,"operator":"GreaterThan","threshold":90,"contactEmails":["you@example.com"],"thresholdType":"Actual"}]'

# Enable cost anomaly alerts (detects sudden spending spikes)
az costmanagement alert create \
  --name anomaly-alert \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --type Budget
Common Free Tier Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Leaving a B1S VM running 24/7 all month
750 hours consumed in ~31 days; a second VM in the same month is fully charged
Configure auto-shutdown at 20:00 daily; start only when working
Creating a Standard tier storage account
Standard_LRS is free; ZRS, GRS, or Premium SkUs are charged
Always use Standard_LRS for free-tier storage
Enabling Azure SQL DTU above S0
S0 (10 DTU) is free; S1 and above are charged
Stay at S0; for heavier workloads use Cosmos DB free tier
Running App Service on B1 instead of F1
B1 is the first paid tier; F1 is free
Verify plan SKU is F1; B1 costs ~$13/month
Creating a second Cosmos DB free-tier account
Only one free-tier Cosmos DB per subscription is allowed
Use multiple databases/containers within one account
Leaving load balancers or static IP addresses allocated
Standard Load Balancer and Public IPs have idle charges
Delete resources not actively in use; use dynamic IP where possible
First 15 GB outbound is free; excess is charged per GB
Monitor outbound bandwidth; use CDN for static content delivery
Credit expires at 30 days regardless of balance
Plan experiments in the first 30 days; upgrade to PAYG before day 30 if needed
Automating Free-Tier Resource Lifecycle with Azure Automation

Azure Automation Account has a generous always-free tier: 500 minutes of job runtime per month. Use it to automatically start and stop VMs on a schedule, ensuring you never waste free VM hours overnight.

# Create an Automation Account (management plane is free)
az automation account create \
  --name auto-free-lab \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Free

# Enable system-assigned managed identity for the automation account
az automation account update \
  --name auto-free-lab \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --assign-identity '[system]'

# Get the managed identity object ID
MI_OID=$(az automation account show \
  --name auto-free-lab \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --query identity.principalId -o tsv)

# Grant VM Contributor role so the automation can start/stop VMs
az role assignment create \
  --role 'Virtual Machine Contributor' \
  --assignee $MI_OID \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id>

# Create a runbook to stop all VMs in a resource group
az automation runbook create \
  --name stop-free-vms \
  --automation-account-name auto-free-lab \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --type PowerShell

# Schedule it to run every day at 20:00 UTC
az automation schedule create \
  --name daily-stop-8pm \
  --automation-account-name auto-free-lab \
  --resource-group rg-free-lab-eastus \
  --frequency Day \
  --interval 1 \
  --start-time $(date -d 'tomorrow 20:00' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S)

Automation job runtime above 500 minutes/month is charged at $0.002/minute. For a simple start/stop runbook that runs in under 1 minute, the 500-minute free quota covers 500 executions per month — more than enough for daily scheduling.
After 12 months, time-limited free services revert to PAYG rates. Azure sends an email notification before the expiry, but it is good practice to track the date yourself.

# Get account creation date (approximation via subscription creation date)
az account show --query '{Created:id}' -o tsv

# More accurate: check via the subscription details REST API
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>?api-version=2022-12-01' \
  --query '{Name:displayName, QuotaId:subscriptionPolicies.quotaId}'

# QuotaId 'FreeTrial' = active free account
# QuotaId 'PayAsYouGo_2014-09-01' = upgraded / PAYG

# View billing period to determine when 12-month services expire
az billing period list --query '[0].{Name:name, Start:billingPeriodStartDate, End:billingPeriodEndDate}' -o table

# Compare current spend to the $200 credit (visible in Cost Analysis in portal)
# Via CLI: view usage since account creation
az consumption usage list \
  --start-date <account-creation-date> \
  --end-date $(date +%Y-%m-%d) \
  --query 'sum([].pretaxCost)' -o tsv
Credit balance in Cost Management portal
Whether spending limit should be removed before services stop
Monthly usage against free limits (VM hours, storage GB, SQL DTU)
Check invoices; upgrade to reserved instances for VM cost savings

The Azure Free Account provides $200 in credits for 30 days, 12 months of popular services (B1S VMs, SQL Database, Blob Storage), and a set of always-free services with no expiry. The spending limit prevents charges while the credit is active but is a one-way door — disabling it upgrades to PAYG. Monitor VM hours carefully against the 750-hour limit, configure auto-shutdown, and set a budget alert immediately after upgrading. Always-free services — Functions, App Service F1, Cosmos DB free tier, AKS management — remain free indefinitely and are unaffected by the 12-month window or any upgrade.
